1: A type: “Son of man, eat what is before you — eat this scroll, then go and speak to the house of Israel.” The command to eat the scroll before speaking is the prophetic-internalization type: the word must be received before it can be given. Jesus embodies this most fully: he is the Word who has always been the word, so there is no gap between reception and proclamation. “The words that I speak to you I do not speak on my own authority, but the Father who dwells in me does his works” (John 14:10) — Jesus speaks from infinite internalization of the Father’s word.
2: A structural note: Ezekiel opens his mouth and receives the scroll. The obedient reception of the divine word — without question or delay — is the prototype of Jesus’s immediate obedience to the Father’s commission: “the Son of Man came not to be served but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many” (Mark 10:45). The opened mouth that receives the divine word is the posture of total readiness for the prophetic mission.
3: A type: “In my mouth it was as sweet as honey.” The word of lament sweet as honey in the prophet’s mouth anticipates Psalms 119:103 (“how sweet are your words to my taste, sweeter than honey to my mouth”) and Revelation 10:9-10, where the angel gives John a scroll that is “sweet as honey in my mouth, but when I had eaten it my stomach became bitter.” Christ himself is described as the one in whom all divine wisdom and knowledge are hidden (Colossians 2:3) — consuming his word is consuming the sweetness of divine truth, even when the content is judgment.
4: A structural note: “Go to the house of Israel and deliver my words to them.” The commission to deliver YHWH’s words is fulfilled ultimately in Christ, who is the Word delivered to Israel. John 1:11 summarizes the entire pattern: “he came to his own, and his own did not receive him.” Every prophetic commission to Israel is a partial anticipation of the final and complete Word-giving in the incarnation.
5: A structural note: “You are not being sent to a people of foreign speech and a difficult language, but to the house of Israel.” The fact that Israel — who already possesses the covenant and YHWH’s words — is harder to reach than foreigners would have been is the OT diagnosis of covenant privilege without transformation. Jesus makes the same point: “many will come from east and west and recline at table with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob in the kingdom of heaven, while the sons of the kingdom will be thrown into outer darkness” (Matthew 8:11-12).
6: A pattern: “If I had sent you to them, they would have listened.” The hypothetical Gentile receptiveness to YHWH’s word — greater than Israel’s actual resistance — prefigures Jesus’s ironic comparisons: the Queen of the South came to hear Solomon, and Nineveh repented at Jonah’s preaching, yet something greater than Solomon and Jonah is here (Matthew 12:41-42). Covenant privilege becomes covenant accountability.
7: A pattern: “The house of Israel will not listen to you, because they will not listen to me.” The rejection of the prophet is the rejection of YHWH — the principle Jesus explicitly applies to himself: “whoever hears you hears me, and whoever rejects you rejects me” (Luke 10:16); “if they hated me, they will also hate you” (John 15:18). The chain of rejection — YHWH → prophet → apostle — means that Israel’s consistent pattern of rejection reaches its climax in the rejection of the Son.
8: A type: “I have made your face as hard as their faces, and your forehead as hard as their foreheads.” The hardening of Ezekiel’s face to match Israel’s stubbornness is the prophetic preparation for sustained resistance. Isaiah 50:7 applies the same language to the Servant: “I have set my face like a flint; I know I will not be put to shame.” Luke 9:51 says Jesus “set his face to go to Jerusalem,” using the same set-face language. The prophet and the Servant both receive a divine hardening for the mission of bearing witness before the resistant.
9: A type: “I have made your forehead like adamant, harder than flint.” The adamantine forehead given to Ezekiel for his mission is the OT image of prophetic determination before opposition. Jesus’s determination toward Jerusalem — proceeding despite knowing the cross awaits — is the ultimate fulfillment of this prophetic resolve. The forehead harder than flint becomes the face set toward the skull-place where the final prophetic word would be delivered.
10: A pattern: “Son of man, take all my words into your heart and hear them with your ears.” The command to internalize the divine word completely — heart and ears — precedes mission. Jesus as the incarnate Word has not merely internalized the Father’s words but is the Father’s Word; there is no distinction in his case between reception and being. The command to Ezekiel points to what is true of Christ by nature.
11: A pattern: “Go to the exiles, to your own people. Speak to them and say, “Thus says the Lord GOD” — whether they listen or not.” The mission to the exiles, Ezekiel’s own displaced people, is a type of Christ’s mission to humanity in its condition of exile from Eden and from the Father. The gospel is the word spoken in the condition of exile — in the far country — calling the scattered to return (Luke 15:11-24).
12: A type: “Then the Spirit lifted me up, and behind me I heard a great rumbling sound: ‘Blessed be the glory of the LORD from his place!’” The Spirit’s lifting of the prophet and the liturgical acclamation of YHWH’s glory anticipates the ascension: the Spirit lifts Jesus into the heavenly realm (Acts 1:9), and the angels’ acclamation of the ascended Christ echoes the creatures’ and wheels’ proclamation of divine glory. “Worthy is the Lamb who was slain to receive power and wealth and wisdom and might and honor and glory and blessing” (Revelation 5:12).
13: A structural note: The great roar of the creatures’ wings and the wheels — the sound of the divine chariot in motion — is the sound of YHWH acting in history. Every divine action in history is the chariot-movement of the Spirit. The incarnation is the chariot’s most dramatic earthward movement; the parousia will be its final and most thunderous.
14: A pattern: “I went in bitterness and grief of spirit, with the hand of the LORD resting powerfully upon me.” Ezekiel’s grief over his mission to rebellious Israel is the prophetic pattern of Christ’s sorrow over Jerusalem: “O Jerusalem, Jerusalem... how often would I have gathered your children together as a hen gathers her brood under her wings, and you were not willing” (Matthew 23:37). The prophetic grief is not weakness but faithfulness — feeling the weight of the mission before the resistant.
15: A pattern: “I sat where they sat and remained among them for seven days, overwhelmed.” Ezekiel’s seven-day silent solidarity with the exiles at Tel-abib — sitting where they sat, sharing their condition before speaking — is the prophetic pattern of incarnational identification. Christ did not speak to humanity from a distance but entered human conditions fully: born in poverty, living as a refugee, dying with criminals. The prophet who sits where the people sit anticipates the God who “became flesh and dwelt among us” (John 1:14).
16: A structural note: After the seven days of silent solidarity, the word of YHWH comes to Ezekiel. The seven-day waiting period reflects the creation week — after the period of formation comes the word of purpose. Jesus’s thirty years of hidden life in Nazareth precede his public ministry: the word is not given until the time of preparation is complete.
17: A type: “Son of man, I have made you a watchman for the house of Israel.” The watchman commission is one of the OT’s most powerful prophetic images — the person whose function is to warn the city of approaching danger, responsible for the blood of those who die for lack of warning. Christ is the ultimate watchman: his eschatological discourse (Mark 13) is the great watchman’s cry, calling Israel and the world to watch for the approach of the day of the Lord. The exhortation “watch and pray” (Mark 14:38; Matthew 25:13) is Christ exercising his watchman function.
18: A type: “If I say to the wicked, ‘You will certainly die,’ and you give no warning... that wicked person will die for his sin, but I will require their blood from you.” The watchman’s blood-accountability for failure to warn establishes the moral weight of prophetic silence. Jesus fulfills the watchman commission absolutely — he never failed to warn, never accommodated the powerful to avoid confrontation (Matthew 23:1-36), and his blood shed on the cross discharges all the blood-accountability accumulated by every prophet who ever warned and was not heeded.
19: A structural note: The watchman who warns faithfully has saved himself even if the warned person dies. Christ’s faithfulness in his prophetic mission is the ground of his vindication — even when his words were rejected and he was executed, he had discharged the prophetic commission completely. The resurrection vindicates the rejected watchman.
20: A type: “If a righteous person turns from their righteousness and commits sin, and I place a stumbling block before them, they will die.” The stumbling block (michshol) placed by YHWH before the apostate righteous person connects to Paul’s use of the stumbling block image for Christ himself: “Christ crucified, a stumbling block to Jews” (1 Corinthians 1:23); “a stone of stumbling and a rock of offense” (1 Peter 2:8, citing Isaiah 8:14). Christ becomes the stumbling block that separates those who persist in apostasy from those who stumble onto repentance.
21: A structural note: The watchman who warns the righteous against sin — and is heeded — has saved the righteous person. Christ’s warnings of judgment in the Gospels are preventive medicine for the covenant community: “if your right eye causes you to sin, tear it out and throw it away” (Matthew 5:29). The drastic preventive warning is the watchman’s mode of saving the city from the approaching army.
22: A type: “The hand of the LORD came upon me there, and he said to me: Get up and go out to the valley, and I will speak with you there.” YHWH summons Ezekiel to a second encounter outside the inhabited space — the valley. The valley encounter repeats the pattern of the initial call-vision: divine initiative, unexpected location, overwhelming presence. Jesus regularly withdraws to unpopulated spaces for encounters with the Father (Mark 1:35; Luke 6:12) — the prophetic pattern of meeting God outside the crowd.
23: A type: “The glory of the LORD was standing there, just like the glory I had seen by the River Chebar, and I fell on my face.” The standing glory of YHWH in the valley and Ezekiel’s prostration anticipates the transfiguration (Matthew 17:6: the disciples “fell on their faces and were terrified”) and John’s prostration before the exalted Christ (Revelation 1:17: “I fell at his feet as though dead”). The glory of YHWH that appears to Ezekiel is the same glory that shines from the face of Jesus Christ (2 Corinthians 4:6).
24: A structural note: “The Spirit entered into me and set me on my feet. He spoke to me and said, ‘Go into your house and shut yourself inside.’” The Spirit-empowered standing followed by an instruction of strategic withdrawal — go inside, wait — echoes Christ’s pattern of withdrawal before major actions (before the feeding of the 5000, John 6:15; before the last supper, Luke 22:8-12). Divine strategy sometimes requires withdrawal before the next forward movement.
25: A structural note: “Cords will be put on you, and you will be tied with them so you cannot go out among the people.” Ezekiel’s binding — prevented from free movement among the people — is the prophetic type of Christ’s binding at his arrest (“they bound him and led him to Pilate,” Matthew 27:2). In both cases, the binding is not defeat but the constraint through which the divine purpose is accomplished: Ezekiel’s immobility makes him a sign; Christ’s binding leads to the cross.
26: A type: “I will make your tongue stick to the roof of your mouth, and you will be silent.” Ezekiel’s prophetic silencing — able to speak only when YHWH opens his mouth — is the extreme form of the prophet’s total dependence on the divine word. Christ’s silence before Pilate (“he gave him no answer, not even to a single charge,” Matthew 27:14) is the voluntary fulfillment of the Servant’s silence: “like a sheep before its shearers is silent, so he did not open his mouth” (Isaiah 53:7). Prophetic silence is the most powerful word when the time for speech is past.
27: A type: “Whenever I speak to you, I will open your mouth, and you will say to them, ‘Thus says the Lord GOD.’” The opened mouth of the prophet — the antithesis of his silencing — is the Spirit’s gift of proclamation. At Pentecost, the disciples’ mouths are opened by the Spirit (Acts 2:4) and they begin to speak in languages they did not know — the ultimate fulfillment of YHWH opening the prophet’s mouth. Christ promises his disciples a similar opening: “for it is not you who speak, but the Spirit of your Father speaking through you” (Matthew 10:20).
